item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Source: Wikimedia Commons
Author: NASA
License: Public domain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoops, forgot to leave a note while editing, but I changed footnote 4, since, to my knowledge, the genus of a scientific name is always capitalized, and I found Pteropus as the genus of Flying Foxes, rather than the original Pterus.

Great little skip, though! I love a skip that clearly has more story to it than the time-frame covered by the article, but ends at the right point to leave the reader with a satisfying amount of "what happens next?" to speculate about on their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is that altitude per minute change a little unusual for the parts when it's just falling? Thrusters were on for some parts but there were other parts with no forces acting on it except gravity. I don't think I want to get into the math but maybe if someone else wants to they can. It just seemed like it was a slowing rate of descent for a little bit and then that last interval before impact it suddenly had a MUCH bigger rate of change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, thanks for the clarification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ship was annihilated when it impacted the moon. The SCP is an escape pod or shuttle of some sort that ejected just prior to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am a bit confused about this. So the SCP is a damaged alien ship and a alien? The ship is made of anti-matter. The SCP came to be when it came out of, like, hyper space or something attached to another ship that got wrecked, and crashed into the Moon? The ship is stored inside the thing in the picture?1 The alien after going low on food then freezes itself?

I don't know. The details of what I thought some aspects of this SCP made more sense to me. I thought of it as a lone ship in space with a lone alien repairing it to go home.

Still, it captivated me enough for a +1 but I would appreciate a bit of an explanation.

Footnotes
1. If so, it's really small than what I expect, especially the alien.

item.title: Bow down to Zhange
item.content:encoded:
Zhange made a really awesome watercolor painting based on this article. She's crazy talented.

Check it (and her other SCP paintings) out here!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been a while since this was posted but I actually really like this one. I'm a sucker for sad stories and this alien pulls at my heartstrings

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for necroposting, but that m3 to cm3 conversion is wrong.
1 m = 100 cm, so 1 m3 = 1003 cm3 = 1000000 cm3.
The rest was right, you just forgot to multiply by 10000.
243536300 kg * c2 = 2.18879511*1025 J = 5.2313459 petatons of TNT.

WolframAlpha says that's ~44 times the energy of the impact that caused the Chicxulub crater, which is 180km wide and 20km deep.
Even with your rounded-down estimate, it's still ~36 Chicxulubs.
I don't know much about explosions, but I'm not sure the moon would survive that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for hard-scifi-esque skips and this one is very well written. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really moved by this one. It's a stock alien what looks weird and is from far away, with the twist that his ship (and presumably him, I guess) is made from horribly dangerous matter that can't be touched. Just not doing a whole lot for me. I also find it a little convenient that the Foundation happened to have a "Magnetic Recovery Ship" stationed on the moon that was available to deal with this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I beg to differ.
Hide ramblings

The escape craft isn't huge. I think it's reasonable that its interior is visible through a foot-wide opening. That being said, I forgot to make the hatch wide enough for -1 to fit through it, so I changed it to .73 m wide.

The purpose for the hatch's composition is ambiguous. I don't think it's made of glass so that the Foundation can see through it, but perhaps so that something can see through it. It also hints that the craft wasn't designed with -1 in mind, but the Foundation doesn't have enough information to conclude this. The backstory is intentionally vague.

There's not much dust in the moon's vicinity at all. Some scientists hypothesize that lunar regolith particles may rise meters or kilometers above the surface via electrostatic levitation before falling back down, but it has never been confirmed. Even if the "Moon fountain" hypothesis is correct, I doubt much damage would have been caused until the vessel neared the surface. And it was falling to the surface pretty quick before it exploded. And for what it's worth there is a tail of sodium atoms in the Moon's wake, but it's pretty dang thin. Anyway, it is generally held that the Moon is surrounded by a vacuum. Its atmosphere is negligible.

05:13 Extraterrestrial vessel appears in flash of white light at an altitude of approximately 130,000 m above far side of moon.

The craft did spontaneously appear near the moon. And it appeared with critical damage, like it had jumped away from a violent confrontation. It appeared and crashed and its escape pod was brought back to Lunar Site-13 within around 15 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it kind of difficult to believe that the entire interior of the ship could be seen from many angles through a one-foot-wide window. Also, why was the hatch made of some kind of glass (sorry, anti-glass), while the rest of the ship is an opaque anti-alloy? If -1 has no photoreception, why would it need a transparent hatch? I get that this is not its purpose. But then…what is its purpose? To provide a convenient way for the Foubdation to see into it? I would recommend making the whole pod anti-glass, or figure out some way for the scientists to see the interior without using a convenient window and removing said window.

Also, there is enough space dust around the moon to cause considerably more damage to the vessel and the pod. And, unless the craft spontaneously appeared near the moon (which is improbable, as -1 had only a few provisions left to bring on to the pod), then it would have run into even more cosmic dust and cause even more damage. I am not sure how to fix this, sorry.

e: Kudos on the science, but when you take that route you need to commit and make sure it's air-tight (pun unintended).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm upvoting for accurate science (I always upvote for good physics) but it isn't much more than a dude in space. But sometimes an SCP is a vehicle for enjoying the containment procedures.

Keep up the scientifically accurate work, friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, you got me there. I think I got my kilotons and megatons confused, but the explosion would be well within the gigatons. There's a decent amount of wiggle room with the test mass I used, since the vessel already had chunks missing and had interior space, so I shoved the explosive power up to 430 gigatons (.43 teratons).

The size of the crater should be fine. For comparison, the Chicxulub crater is 180 km in width and 20 km in depth, and the energy of the impact that created it is around 100 teratons. That's a big boom.

Some calculations I did with an impact energy calculator showed my crater is well within the ballpark for a 430 gigaton boom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, this is what the z-machine looks like in operation.

Somewhere, a mad scientist is quietly, um, enjoying themselves to this picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Given the foundation's budget and some near-future technology 1kT's totally doable.

We've built 1 kT+ electromagnets, but they only work at a burst because you have to keep them cool while pumping a huge amount of power through them. The largest such device is the z-pulsed power facility, aka the Z-machine, aka the closest thing to a wave motion gun mankind has yet built. (In addition to being used for studying fusion reactions and generating massive magnetic fields and X-ray beams, it can shoot small metal plates at 3x escape velocity 8D) Also, this is what the z-machine looks like in operation. It has been scientifically proven to be one of the most unrelentingly awesome machines ever constructed.

Anyway, you'd need one of these, but replacing the tungsten wire arrays with a highish temperature superconductor, and hooked up to something like a fusion reactor. But yeah, doable without dragging out too much magitek.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm more concerned about the damaged vessel hitting the moon - the article listed it as being in the kT range, but some quick math suggests that… No, it's gonna be bigger.

Let's see: assuming 100% matter to energy conversion, that gives us 24,353.63 Kg * c2 = 2.1887951 * 1021 Joules, or about a 0.5 teraton explosion. Yoikes.

Now, what we're all wondering: will this blow up the moon? …Not quite.

The gravitational binding energy of the moon is estimated at ~1029 Joules, or 8 orders of magnitude greater than our antimatter bomb-ship can provide even at 100% conversion.

It'd leave a hell of a crater, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not an expert, but a quick glimpse around the web seems to say that there is such a thing as a kilotesla field, but a stable one existing only works on paper with current technology we have to work with. So yes and no. There's nothing that says they couldn't be manufactured, we just need to figure it out.
What I meant to say before (though I was being sassy about it, so apologies good sir) is that it's sci-fi. Standard use amnestics are a thing in this world, they have an undetectable moon base in this document, so as far as writing goes I think you can give them the extra mile in technology and say yes the Foundation can have a stable 1 kT field. But what with the lenient canon and whatnot, if you want to say otherwise then write otherwise.

I dunno man, I'm bad at explaining things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If they can do whatever they want then there aren't any challenges to containing objects. That gets pretty boring very fast, IMO.

At any rate are you saying that there is no such thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe the answer is the SCP Foundation can do what they want because they're the SCP Foundation and they have about 1% of the world kept secret from everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do sustainable 1 kT magnets exist? I was working on something that also needs really powerful magnetic fields and my research said no. Was I wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: whelp I got bitch slapped by science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, but the one I tried writing is more like this article (anti-matter ship). I do concede that this version is better than mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good to see this one up and not being plunged into the depths of negative downvotes.
Nicely done, Lesh.

item.title: Re: Very scientific.
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I'm glad you liked it.

The idea is that the craft, despite its hazardous composition, can be contained indefinitely. It's out of fuel, so there isn't anything the creature inside it can do to breach containment. Even if the power went offline and both backup reactors failed and the field generators turned off and the craft contacted the interior of the containment unit, it would just cause an explosion. A gargantuan explosion, but still just an explosion. And that would be it. One boom. It's just stored on the Moon because transporting it to the Earth's surface would involve unnecessary risk.

I think that would make it Safe. The reason it's Euclid is because of the dude inside it.

Eventually, when the poor guy comes out of suspended animation and starves to death, it could probably be downgraded to Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope. It wouldn't have exploded until it contacted the lunar surface. The density of interplanetary space in the vicinity of Earth is approximately five particles per cubic centimeter. In order to annihilate, there needs to be one particle for every antiparticle present.

Let's try a little thought experiment.

MATH MATH MATH
WOO CANCELING UNITS

An ellipsoid the same size as the damaged alien vessel would have a volume of 32044.25 m3 or 3204425 cm3. For the sake of simplicity, let's say our test ellipsoid is solid and made of low alloy steel. Low alloy steel has a density of 7.60 g/cm3. So our ellipsoid has a mass of 24353630 g. One gram of iron has roughly 1.1x1022 iron atoms. And an individual iron atom (let's go with the most common iron isotope, 56Fe) is composed of 82 particles (30 neutrons, 26 protons, 26 electrons).

So, roughly, the total number of particles in our test ellipsoid is about (24353630 g) x (1.1x1022 atoms/g) x(82 particles/atom) = 2.1966974 x 1031 particles or 21,966,974,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 particles.

The same volume of interplanetary space would have about 16,022,125 particles. The alien vessel would degrade over time as it moved through the interplanetary medium, but it would take a very, very, very long time to be destroyed. The effect within the article's timeline is negligible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are several already. One of them switches all the matter in the universe into antimatter and vice-versa on a regular basis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I once wrote an anti-matter skip (which failed), but I am glad someone did one that worked out (sort of).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really care for this scip, it just seems like an alien spaceship made out of anti-matter. It doesn't do anything or expand on it, I'm pretty sure that the ship would've exploded already since space isn't a perfect vacuum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm neutral on this one but tending toward upvote territory. I dunno. Tone etc is pretty much spot on, but it is still just a space bloke in space, "stranger in a strange land" and all that. It didn't reel me in.

No vote I'm afraid, but as I say it's just shy of an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-163, meet your Series III brother, SCP-2226.

Seriously, this is 163 turned up to 11 in a good way. I mean, 163 can at least socialize with other skips, can interact with researchers. This thing must be left in a tin can because, if it isn't, it will die, and that's tragic.

item.title: Very scientific.
item.content:encoded:
I'm brand new myself, so I don't want to act like an expert, but this is very well-written in my opinion. It just has the right tone to it- clinical and sort of dry, describing it in terms that are painstakingly specific and yet somehow make it feel sort of vague. I get the impression you were very meticulous in making this read a certain way. This is the sort of thing people have conspiracy theories about, I think. Earned my upvote.

Aside from that, my one question is, why the Euclid classification? Seems like a craft made out of antimatter that could have "catastrophic consequences" on containment breach and has to be stored on the goddamn MOON should be classified as a Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's my second SCP after "Letter from Grandma". I hope y'all like it!

Big, huge thanks to CupertinoEffect for his awesome line-by-line critique. Also thanks to azzleflux, bluesoul, and DreadLindwyrm for their feedback.
